Mon, Nov 24, 2025

0:01 - rUv

0:01 - rUv

0:06 - Mark Allen
So I think we're, we're kind of in, it hasn't made enough. Mak really hasn't made enough progress. I think to get Coaching from you today, I think he just got to maybe you can just tell him what it is. We can make this a kind of a super short call, Mak, just to get guidance from Ruv about the process that you're going through right now. Cause I guess there's, there's two different approaches here. One is to just kind of start over and just do the MVP, and two is to try to back out of a bunch of stuff that's been done to get to the MVP. I'm not sure which one's faster and better. Maybe you can suggest on that. I mean, if we kind of start over, we could use best practices from the get-go, but we got to, as you noted, we need to have a clear spec, and I don't think that we have a clear spec on the MVP. I've seen. So Mak said he was just working on it.

1:04 - rUv
You want me to give my opinion first or you want Mak to go?

1:07 - Unidentified Speaker
Please, yeah.

1:09 - rUv
So I think you've got a very ambitious startup that's in the right space at the right time. And you can do really well. The challenge is execution, right? You can build almost anything quickly if you know how to build it. But that doesn't change the fundamental laws of like a capitalistic society. You gotta market, you gotta get users, you gotta have a good user experience, you gotta limit churn and be more profitable than, you know, you gotta make more than you spend, right? Obvious stuff. Now, the challenge that you have is you got into the classic kind of vibe coders dilemma. And I suffer from this just as much as anybody else. Then what happens is you have a tendency to say, oh my God, I can sort of rapidly prototype and create all these new features that makes this the world's best at something. And then you quickly realize that those thousand things I invented all need to be built, tested for regressions and optimized and all the things. And it turns out that you've just made your work a million times more complex and to a point where you you have to build all these things to get to an MVP. So taking a step back, the opportunity isn't to do everything for everyone, boil the ocean, which is what we're doing right now. It's to do one thing really well and prove that one thing and then subsequently add new features as fast as we can on a kind of rapid basis, weekly preferably, and then keep adding stuff once you've got a solid functional core. The challenge we have is that solid functional core. And when you look at the challenges we had last week with using Superbase, we had all these conflicting components for logging in and registering and subscriptions. One thing would break another, and there was all these sort of conflicts. And that's just in the user login itself. That doesn't include the fundamental functionality of the site itself, which also has a lot of interconnected relationships. So back when we originally started, I'm like, remember the demo I put together for you guys? It was like, it was the sub, this is like the thing that the end user needs to use kind of thing and it's minimal. And it was, and generally that would have gotten you to something to show a customer and potentially build them. Then we went to, well, we need to have this kind of dashboard for everything under the sun that the, dentists, I guess, would use.

3:49 - Mark Allen
Well, so I think that the issue is, is Ruth, that we started off, Mak had done a ton of work just on the UI. And so those, those UI components for Magic Pattern had all this garbage in them, like, you know, had mock data as part of the UI, had, it was just UI components. And so, you know, when we tried to use that and work, and that was like the entire app. And so we spent a lot of time building the entire database trying to map that, trying to deal with the security, the zero risk security issue, a whole bunch of stuff that I think we got just caught up in a bird's nest there.","textTemplate":"","endCharIdx":4135,"metadataSeparator":"

3:49 - Mark Allen
Well, so I think that the issue is, is Ruth, that we started off, Mak had done a ton of work just on the UI. And so those, those UI components for Magic Pattern had all this garbage in them, like, you know, had mock data as part of the UI, had, it was just UI components. And so, you know, when we tried to use that and work, and that was like the entire app. And so we spent a lot of time building the entire database trying to map that, trying to deal with the security, the zero risk security issue, a whole bunch of stuff that I think we got just caught up in a bird's nest there. Whereas if instead we kind of started from the beginning and said, okay, you can have a whole PRD, but you say the first thing we're going to build out is this functionality, which is just basically the automated scheduling agent. So the automated scheduling agent is a beautiful first thing to build out because it has an AI integration. It has basically a conversational voice AI that integrates with the app, that integrates through the app to the PMS through that API bridge. So we could just do that. And then we start to build on the rest of the stuff instead of trying to build all of the infrastructure for everything, which is what we'd done. And now we're in a mess. So anyway, we're trying to figure out where to, do we take the mess and try to reverse engineer them that basically clip off what we don't need from the mess or do we just start over?

5:23 - rUv
I don't need to start over. But what I think you need to do is think about your core value proposition. So let's explore this idea of a scheduling agent as the core value proposition. Now, if you were to build the scheduling agent, you would start with with the UI, the UI would come after you built the core scheduling agent. So you'd start with a functional agentic workflow of some sort that does and solves the problem that you're looking to solve from a middleware and database point of view. Like, can I deploy the agent? Does it run? Does it do? And can I do it repeatedly, efficiently, without any errors? Once I've got that middleware in place and basically I can make it work, I'm going to probably apply a simulation to it. How do I scale this business and everything around it using various agentics processes? That essentially allows me to understand that the thing will work without me having to go into the UI and discover it doesn't work, which is what we're doing repeatedly. Basically, the calls we have are us logging into website, looking at the console and realizing that half the things don't work, or at least don't work efficiently. Before we ever get to that, we need to know that the core works. And we can mock, we don't have to mock it, we can actually simulate the entire workflow with real data, with real results, real calendars, and from, you know, and actually operate the business from now for the next 24 months, completely, completely simulated, using real data, saving real databases, calendar appointments, doing real emails. Once we know that the thing works, it's scalable, we can meet the benchmarks for the business we want and scale to a point where we know we're gonna be profitable, X amount of users, then we go and say, okay, let's connect this up to a front end, minimal viable, needs to have a login, needs to have registration, needs to have forgot my password, needs to have some form of social probably, maybe. And then the first thing you see, it needs a list see my calendar, we need to just have that core. The middle works, it's validated, it's been simulated, all the bugs are fixed. We know from a core infrastructure that everything works as expected. Then we can plug on any UI we want later. You can vibe that all day every day because you've got a core API that we know works. So you've moved away from this kind of top-down, I'm going to design it and then I'm going to try to retroactively make it work, which is just awful. And I know you guys are suffering through that. I can see you guys suffering through that to one that's like, well, we have a core that works and is 100% functional and scalable. That's what we need. And I think you actually need to take a step away from your UI for a week or so and focus on the core.","textTemplate":"","startCharIdx":3401,"endCharIdx":7800,"metadataSeparator":"

3:46 - Krysta Fox
Yeah, so I've only done feature flags going forward. So I've, in past projects, used GitHub issues to design what feature can be exposed to the client or hidden from the client. And I've used LaunchDarkly in the past. Which I think is a little bit bloated, but we're curious if there's a more streamlined way. So I think for Mac's project, we want to focus on the ability of feature flagging kind of retroactively now, because a lot of them have been, like a lot of the features are already existing. So I was thinking one way we could do it is, you know, just simply using CSS to hide or display different features to, the user that's obviously not powerful um but uh it and it could get a little bit bloated so I was wondering if you had like inside of cloud flow it sounds like maybe we don't have that right now um the ability to kind of conceptualize well what would it be what would be a feature how could we make that module uh like turn on and turn off The module is exactly the right way to think about it.

5:10 - rUv
Now, the issue you have with CSS is you're loading everything that doesn't need to be loaded and potentially that's security considerations. It's tacky. It's not optimal. It's easy. You're hiding, you know, display none, basically. But the problem with display none is it still renders and it increases the time it takes you to load the page and there's potential security, you know, risk. In things that aren't adequately secured. The other approach is a more modular approach that says I can use an RLS policy. The RLS policy is basically restricting access in the same way you would restrict access based on data itself in the database, right? So we've got a basis of a subscription system and an RLS policy that describes what and how you access the system, how many records or how many things you can post or what you can do. So what we probably want to do is basically, you know, remove. It's pretty easy at this point to remove includes in the rendering of the page and do an MVP of the site that essentially is only the stuff that we're happy to sort of launch with. And then as we do rollouts of additional features, we basically, you know, we'll do a, you know, access-based inclusion of those features. So if you were part of, let's say, a certain email group or you've been assigned admin or whatever access privileges, then it basically gives you those modules and includes those in the rendering or access to the page. That's more secure. You're not probably going to delete.

6:53 - Mak Allen
That would be very useful because admins are going to need to be able to hide various features from different members of staff, depending on what they're comfortable with.

7:06 - rUv
So that's like RBAC, right?

7:10 - rUv
Yeah, exactly. Role-based is basically the sort of user-based interface, and the RLS is the mechanism in which you're defining who's got access to the data itself. So a combination of the two make the modular components available. Scenario, what you might want to do is first define what the things you want to launch with are feature wise. And then secondly, what areas you want to restrict and how you want to restrict them. Or not include them. All right. So let's the site. Let's just look at it quick. I see just the VS code.

8:04 - Krysta Fox
And then Ruv, as an aside, when we do projects going forward, is there a more streamlined way, whether it's using GitHub issues or some other methodology for feature flagging to do this RBAC and RLS?

8:24 - rUv
Well, you probably want to do some kind of development roadmap.

8:30 - Krysta Fox
Yeah, for sure.

8:32 - Mak Allen
So we want the dashboard, we want the Patients tab, and we want the Communications tab, as well as the Settings tab.

8:44 - rUv
All the settings or some subset of settings?","textTemplate":"","startCharIdx":2870,"endCharIdx":6933,"metadataSeparator":"

So I think for Mac's project, we want to focus on the ability of feature flagging kind of retroactively now, because a lot of them have been, like a lot of the features are already existing. 5:10 - rUv

You're hiding, you know, display none, basically. But the problem with display none is it still renders and it increases the time it takes you to load the page and there's potential security, you know, risk. In things that aren't adequately secured. The other approach is a more modular approach that says I can use an RLS policy. The RLS policy is basically restricting access in the same way you would restrict access based on data itself in the database, right? So we've got a basis of a subscription system and an RLS policy that describes what and how you access the system, how many records or how many things you can post or what you can do. So what we probably want to do is basically, you know, remove. It's pretty easy at this point to remove includes in the rendering of the page and do an MVP of the site that essentially is only the stuff that we're happy to sort of launch with. And then as we do rollouts of additional features, we basically, you know, we'll do a, you know, access-based inclusion of those features. So if you were part of, let's say, a certain email group or you've been assigned admin or whatever access privileges, then it basically gives you those modules and includes those in the rendering or access to the page. That's more secure. You're not probably going to delete.

So that's like RBAC, right?

7:10 - rUv

So a combination of the two make the modular components available. Scenario, what you might want to do is first define what the things you want to launch with are feature wise. Ruv Coaching
Mon, Nov 24, 2025

0:01 - rUv
All right.

0:01 - rUv
Yeah. I saw Mak message. So you guys want to start there? Yeah.

0:06 - Mark Allen
So I think we're, we're kind of in, it hasn't made enough. Mak really hasn't made enough progress. I think to get Coaching from you today, I think he just got to maybe you can just tell him what it is. We can make this a kind of a super short call, Mak, just to get guidance from Ruv about the process that you're going through right now. Cause I guess there's, there's two different approaches here. One is to just kind of start over and just do the MVP, and two is to try to back out of a bunch of stuff that's been done to get to the MVP. I'm not sure which one's faster and better. Maybe you can suggest on that. I mean, if we kind of start over, we could use best practices from the get-go, but we got to, as you noted, we need to have a clear spec, and I don't think that we have a clear spec on the MVP. I've seen. So Mak said he was just working on it.

1:04 - rUv
You want me to give my opinion first or you want Mak to go?

1:07 - Unidentified Speaker
Please, yeah.

1:09 - rUv
So I think you've got a very ambitious startup that's in the right space at the right time. And you can do really well. The challenge is execution, right? You can build almost anything quickly if you know how to build it. But that doesn't change the fundamental laws of like a capitalistic society. You gotta market, you gotta get users, you gotta have a good user experience, you gotta limit churn and be more profitable than, you know, you gotta make more than you spend, right? Obvious stuff. Now, the challenge that you have is you got into the classic kind of vibe coders dilemma. And I suffer from this just as much as anybody else. Then what happens is you have a tendency to say, oh my God, I can sort of rapidly prototype and create all these new features that makes this the world's best at something. And then you quickly realize that those thousand things I invented all need to be built, tested for regressions and optimized and all the things. And it turns out that you've just made your work a million times more complex and to a point where you you have to build all these things to get to an MVP. So taking a step back, the opportunity isn't to do everything for everyone, boil the ocean, which is what we're doing right now. It's to do one thing really well and prove that one thing and then subsequently add new features as fast as we can on a kind of rapid basis, weekly preferably, and then keep adding stuff once you've got a solid functional core. The challenge we have is that solid functional core. And when you look at the challenges we had last week with using Superbase, we had all these conflicting components for logging in and registering and subscriptions. One thing would break another, and there was all these sort of conflicts. And that's just in the user login itself. That doesn't include the fundamental functionality of the site itself, which also has a lot of interconnected relationships. So back when we originally started, I'm like, remember the demo I put together for you guys? It was like, it was the sub, this is like the thing that the end user needs to use kind of thing and it's minimal. And it was, and generally that would have gotten you to something to show a customer and potentially build them. Then we went to, well, we need to have this kind of dashboard for everything under the sun that the, dentists, I guess, would use.

3:49 - Mark Allen
Well, so I think that the issue is, is Ruth, that we started off, Mak had done a ton of work just on the UI. And so those, those UI components for Magic Pattern had all this garbage in them, like, you know, had mock data as part of the UI, had, it was just UI components. And so, you know, when we tried to use that and work, and that was like the entire app. And so we spent a lot of time building the entire database trying to map that, trying to deal with the security, the zero risk security issue, a whole bunch of stuff that I think we got just caught up in a bird's nest there.","textTemplate":"","endCharIdx":4135,"metadataSeparator":"
","type":"TEXT","hash":"3p/JQBaBn2W/ow3GgioJaVYyDO8Llh60FUrmcKTdV20="}

3:49 - Mark Allen
Well, so I think that the issue is, is Ruth, that we started off, Mak had done a ton of work just on the UI. And so those, those UI components for Magic Pattern had all this garbage in them, like, you know, had mock data as part of the UI, had, it was just UI components. And so, you know, when we tried to use that and work, and that was like the entire app. And so we spent a lot of time building the entire database trying to map that, trying to deal with the security, the zero risk security issue, a whole bunch of stuff that I think we got just caught up in a bird's nest there. Whereas if instead we kind of started from the beginning and said, okay, you can have a whole PRD, but you say the first thing we're going to build out is this functionality, which is just basically the automated scheduling agent. So the automated scheduling agent is a beautiful first thing to build out because it has an AI integration. It has basically a conversational voice AI that integrates with the app, that integrates through the app to the PMS through that API bridge. So we could just do that. And then we start to build on the rest of the stuff instead of trying to build all of the infrastructure for everything, which is what we'd done. And now we're in a mess. So anyway, we're trying to figure out where to, do we take the mess and try to reverse engineer them that basically clip off what we don't need from the mess or do we just start over?

5:23 - rUv
I don't need to start over. But what I think you need to do is think about your core value proposition. So let's explore this idea of a scheduling agent as the core value proposition. Now, if you were to build the scheduling agent, you would start with with the UI, the UI would come after you built the core scheduling agent. So you'd start with a functional agentic workflow of some sort that does and solves the problem that you're looking to solve from a middleware and database point of view. Like, can I deploy the agent? Does it run? Does it do? And can I do it repeatedly, efficiently, without any errors? Once I've got that middleware in place and basically I can make it work, I'm going to probably apply a simulation to it. How do I scale this business and everything around it using various agentics processes? That essentially allows me to understand that the thing will work without me having to go into the UI and discover it doesn't work, which is what we're doing repeatedly. Basically, the calls we have are us logging into website, looking at the console and realizing that half the things don't work, or at least don't work efficiently. And I've used LaunchDarkly in the past. Which I think is a little bit bloated, but we're curious if there's a more streamlined way. So I think for Mac's project, we want to focus on the ability of feature flagging kind of retroactively now, because a lot of them have been, like a lot of the features are already existing. 5:10 - rUv

You're hiding, you know, display none, basically. But the problem with display none is it still renders and it increases the time it takes you to load the page and there's potential security, you know, risk. In things that aren't adequately secured. The other approach is a more modular approach that says I can use an RLS policy. The RLS policy is basically restricting access in the same way you would restrict access based on data itself in the database, right? So we've got a basis of a subscription system and an RLS policy that describes what and how you access the system, how many records or how many things you can post or what you can do. So what we probably want to do is basically, you know, remove. It's pretty easy at this point to remove includes in the rendering of the page and do an MVP of the site that essentially is only the stuff that we're happy to sort of launch with. We can make this a kind of a super short call, Mak, just to get guidance from Ruv about the process that you're going through right now. Cause I guess there's, there's two different approaches here. One is to just kind of start over and just do the MVP, and two is to try to back out of a bunch of stuff that's been done to get to the MVP. I'm not sure which one's faster and better. Maybe you can suggest on that. I mean, if we kind of start over, we could use best practices from the get-go, but we got to, as you noted, we need to have a clear spec, and I don't think that we have a clear spec on the MVP. I've seen. So Mak said he was just working on it.

1:04 - rUv
You want me to give my opinion first or you want Mak to go?

Please, yeah.

1:09 - rUv
So I think you've got a very ambitious startup that's in the right space at the right time. And you can do really well. The challenge is execution, right? You can build almost anything quickly if you know how to build it. But that doesn't change the fundamental laws of like a capitalistic society. You gotta market, you gotta get users, you gotta have a good user experience, you gotta limit churn and be more profitable than, you know, you gotta make more than you spend, right? Obvious stuff. Now, the challenge that you have is you got into the classic kind of vibe coders dilemma. And I suffer from this just as much as anybody else. Then what happens is you have a tendency to say, oh my God, I can sort of rapidly prototype and create all these new features that makes this the world's best at something. And then you quickly realize that those thousand things I invented all need to be built, tested for regressions and optimized and all the things. And it turns out that you've just made your work a million times more complex and to a point where you you have to build all these things to get to an MVP. So taking a step back, the opportunity isn't to do everything for everyone, boil the ocean, which is what we're doing right now. It's to do one thing really well and prove that one thing and then subsequently add new features as fast as we can on a kind of rapid basis, weekly preferably, and then keep adding stuff once you've got a solid functional core. The challenge we have is that solid functional core. And when you look at the challenges we had last week with using Superbase, we had all these conflicting components for logging in and registering and subscriptions. One thing would break another, and there was all these sort of conflicts. And that's just in the user login itself. That doesn't include the fundamental functionality of the site itself, which also has a lot of interconnected relationships. So back when we originally started, I'm like, remember the demo I put together for you guys? It was like, it was the sub, this is like the thing that the end user needs to use kind of thing and it's minimal. And it was, and generally that would have gotten you to something to show a customer and potentially build them. Then we went to, well, we need to have this kind of dashboard for everything under the sun that the, dentists, I guess, would use.

3:49 - Mark Allen
Well, so I think that the issue is, is Ruth, that we started off, Mak had done a ton of work just on the UI. And so those, those UI components for Magic Pattern had all this garbage in them, like, you know, had mock data as part of the UI, had, it was just UI components. And so, you know, when we tried to use that and work, and that was like the entire app. And so we spent a lot of time building the entire database trying to map that, trying to deal with the security, the zero risk security issue, a whole bunch of stuff that I think we got just caught up in a bird's nest there.","textTemplate":"","endCharIdx":4135,"metadataSeparator":"

5:23 - rUv

Now, if you were to build the scheduling agent, you would start with with the UI, the UI would come after you built the core scheduling agent. Like, can I deploy the agent? Does it run? Does it do? And can I do it repeatedly, efficiently, without any errors? How do I scale this business and everything around it using various agentics processes? Basically, the calls we have are us logging into website, looking at the console and realizing that half the things don't work, or at least don't work efficiently. Before we ever get to that, we need to know that the core works. And we can mock, we don't have to mock it, we can actually simulate the entire workflow with real data, with real results, real calendars, and from, you know, and actually operate the business from now for the next 24 months, completely, completely simulated, using real data, saving real databases, calendar appointments, doing real emails. Once we know that the thing works, it's scalable, we can meet the benchmarks for the business we want and scale to a point where we know we're gonna be profitable, X amount of users, then we go and say, okay, let's connect this up to a front end, minimal viable, needs to have a login, needs to have registration, needs to have forgot my password, needs to have some form of social probably, maybe. And then the first thing you see, it needs a list see my calendar, we need to just have that core. The middle works, it's validated, it's been simulated, all the bugs are fixed. We know from a core infrastructure that everything works as expected. Then we can plug on any UI we want later. You can vibe that all day every day because you've got a core API that we know works. So you've moved away from this kind of top-down, I'm going to design it and then I'm going to try to retroactively make it work, which is just awful. And I know you guys are suffering through that. I can see you guys suffering through that to one that's like, well, we have a core that works and is 100% functional and scalable. That's what we need. What do you mean, feature flags? Marissa, you want to jump in here?

3:37 - Krysta Fox
Oh, Ruben, this is the first time you're hearing of feature flags?

3:43 - rUv
No, I'm just wondering how you want to implement them.

3:46 - Krysta Fox
Yeah, so I've only done feature flags going forward. So I've, in past projects, used GitHub issues to design what feature can be exposed to the client or hidden from the client. And I've used LaunchDarkly in the past. Which I think is a little bit bloated, but we're curious if there's a more streamlined way. So I think for Mac's project, we want to focus on the ability of feature flagging kind of retroactively now, because a lot of them have been, like a lot of the features are already existing. So I was thinking one way we could do it is, you know, just simply using CSS to hide or display different features to, the user that's obviously not powerful um but uh it and it could get a little bit bloated so I was wondering if you had like inside of cloud flow it sounds like maybe we don't have that right now um the ability to kind of conceptualize well what would it be what would be a feature how could we make that module uh like turn on and turn off The module is exactly the right way to think about it.

5:10 - rUv
Now, the issue you have with CSS is you're loading everything that doesn't need to be loaded and potentially that's security considerations. It's tacky. It's not optimal. It's easy. You're hiding, you know, display none, basically. But the problem with display none is it still renders and it increases the time it takes you to load the page and there's potential security, you know, risk. In things that aren't adequately secured. The other approach is a more modular approach that says I can use an RLS policy. The RLS policy is basically restricting access in the same way you would restrict access based on data itself in the database, right? So we've got a basis of a subscription system and an RLS policy that describes what and how you access the system, how many records or how many things you can post or what you can do. So what we probably want to do is basically, you know, remove. It's pretty easy at this point to remove includes in the rendering of the page and do an MVP of the site that essentially is only the stuff that we're happy to sort of launch with. And then as we do rollouts of additional features, we basically, you know, we'll do a, you know, access-based inclusion of those features. So if you were part of, let's say, a certain email group or you've been assigned admin or whatever access privileges, then it basically gives you those modules and includes those in the rendering or access to the page. That's more secure. You're not probably going to delete.

6:53 - Mak Allen
That would be very useful because admins are going to need to be able to hide various features from different members of staff, depending on what they're comfortable with.

7:06 - rUv
So that's like RBAC, right?

7:10 - rUv

And I've used LaunchDarkly in the past. Which I think is a little bit bloated, but we're curious if there's a more streamlined way. So I think for Mac's project, we want to focus on the ability of feature flagging kind of retroactively now, because a lot of them have been, like a lot of the features are already existing. So I was thinking one way we could do it is, you know, just simply using CSS to hide or display different features to, the user that's obviously not powerful um but uh it and it could get a little bit bloated so I was wondering if you had like inside of cloud flow it sounds like maybe we don't have that right now um the ability to kind of conceptualize well what would it be what would be a feature how could we make that module uh like turn on and turn off The module is exactly the right way to think about it.

5:10 - rUv
Now, the issue you have with CSS is you're loading everything that doesn't need to be loaded and potentially that's security considerations. It's tacky. It's not optimal. It's easy. You're hiding, you know, display none, basically. But the problem with display none is it still renders and it increases the time it takes you to load the page and there's potential security, you know, risk. In things that aren't adequately secured. The other approach is a more modular approach that says I can use an RLS policy. The RLS policy is basically restricting access in the same way you would restrict access based on data itself in the database, right? So we've got a basis of a subscription system and an RLS policy that describes what and how you access the system, how many records or how many things you can post or what you can do. So what we probably want to do is basically, you know, remove. It's pretty easy at this point to remove includes in the rendering of the page and do an MVP of the site that essentially is only the stuff that we're happy to sort of launch with. And then as we do rollouts of additional features, we basically, you know, we'll do a, you know, access-based inclusion of those features. So if you were part of, let's say, a certain email group or you've been assigned admin or whatever access privileges, then it basically gives you those modules and includes those in the rendering or access to the page. That's more secure. You're not probably going to delete.

6:53 - Mak Allen
That would be very useful because admins are going to need to be able to hide various features from different members of staff, depending on what they're comfortable with.

7:06 - rUv
So that's like RBAC, right?

7:10 - rUv
Yeah, exactly. Role-based is basically the sort of user-based interface, and the RLS is the mechanism in which you're defining who's got access to the data itself. So a combination of the two make the modular components available. Scenario, what you might want to do is first define what the things you want to launch with are feature wise.
